title: TOWARDS A UNIFIED VIEW OF PARAMETER-EFFICIENT TRANSFER LEARNING [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## TOWARDS A UNIFIED VIEW OF PARAMETER-EFFICIENT TRANSFER LEARNING

## **Junxian He** _[∗]_

Carnegie Mellon University junxianh@cs.cmu.edu

**Chunting Zhou** _[∗]_ Carnegie Mellon University chuntinz@cs.cmu.edu

## **Xuezhe Ma**

University of Southern California xuezhema@isi.edu

**Taylor Berg-Kirkpatrick** UC San Diego tberg@eng.ucsd.edu

**Graham Neubig** Carnegie Mellon University gneubig@cs.cmu.edu

## ABSTRACT

Fine-tuning large pretrained language models on downstream tasks has become the de-facto learning paradigm in NLP. However, conventional approaches finetune all the parameters of the pretrained model, which becomes prohibitive as the model size and the number of tasks grow. Recent work has proposed a variety of parameter-efficient transfer learning methods that only fine-tune a small number of (extra) parameters to attain strong performance. While effective, the critical ingredients for success and the connections among the various methods are poorly understood. In this paper, we break down the design of state-of-the-art parameter-efficient transfer learning methods and present a unified framework that establishes connections between them. Specifically, we re-frame them as modifications to specific hidden states in pretrained models, and define a set of design dimensions along which different methods vary, such as the function to compute the modification and the position to apply the modification. Through comprehensive empirical studies across machine translation, text summarization, language understanding, and text classification benchmarks, we utilize the unified view to identify important design choices in previous methods. Furthermore, our unified framework enables the transfer of design elements across different approaches, and as a result we are able to instantiate new parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods that tune less parameters than previous methods while being more effective, achieving comparable results to fine-tuning all parameters on all four tasks.[1]

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Transfer learning from pre-trained language models (PLMs) is now the prevalent paradigm in natural language processing, yielding strong performance on many tasks (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2020). The most common way to adapt general-purpose PLMs to downstream tasks is to fine-tune all the model parameters ( _full fine-tuning_ ). However, this results in a separate copy of fine-tuned model parameters for each task, which is prohibitively expensive when serving models that perform a large number of tasks. This issue is particularly salient with the ever-increasing size of PLMs, which now range from hundreds of millions (Radford et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020) to hundreds of billions (Brown et al., 2020) or even trillions of parameters (Fedus et al., 2021).

To mitigate this issue, a few lightweight alternatives have been proposed to update only a small number of extra parameters while keeping most pretrained parameters frozen. For example, _adapter tuning_ (Houlsby et al., 2019) inserts small neural modules called adapters to each layer of the pretrained network and only the adapters are trained at fine-tuning time. Inspired by the success of prompting methods that control PLMs through textual prompts (Brown et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021a), _prefix tuning_ (Li & Liang, 2021) and _prompt tuning_ (Lester et al., 2021) prepend an additional _l_ tunable

Figure 2: Performance of different methods on the XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) summarization task. The number of fine-tuned parameters is relative to the tuned parameters in full fine-tuning.

prefix tokens to the input or hidden layers and only train these soft prompts when fine-tuning on downstream tasks. More recently, Hu et al. (2021) learn low-rank matrices to approximate parameter updates. We illustrate these methods in Figure 1. These approaches have all been reported to demonstrate comparable performance to full fine-tuning on different sets of tasks, often through updating less than 1% of the original model parameters. Besides parameter savings, parameter-efficient tuning makes it possible to quickly adapt to new tasks without catastrophic forgetting (Pfeiffer et al., 2021) and often exhibits superior robustness in out-of-distribution evaluation (Li & Liang, 2021).

However, we contend that the important ingredients that contribute to the success of these parameterefficient tuning methods are poorly understood, and the connections between them are still unclear. In this paper, we aim to answer three questions: (1) How are these methods connected? (2) Do these methods share design elements that are essential for their effectiveness, and what are they? (3) Can the effective ingredients of each method be transferred to others to yield more effective variants?

In order to answer these questions, we first derive an alternative form of prefix tuning that reveals prefix tuning’s close connections with adapters (§3.1). Based on this we then devise a unified framework that frames the aforementioned methods as different ways to modify the hidden representations of frozen PLMs (§3.2). Our unified framework decomposes previous methods along a _shared_ set of design dimensions, such as the function used to perform the modification, the position in which to impose this modification, and how to integrate the modification. This framework allows us to transfer design choices across approaches to propose new variants such as adapters with multiple heads (§3.3). In experiments, we first show that existing parameter-efficient tuning methods still lag behind full fine-tuning on higher-resource and challenging tasks (§4.2), as exemplified in Figure 2. Then we utilize the unified framework to identify critical design choices and validate the proposed variants empirically (§4.3-4.6). Our experiments on four NLP benchmarks covering text summarization, machine translation (MT), text classification, and general language understanding, demonstrate that the proposed variant uses less parameters than existing methods while being more effective, matching full fine-tuning results on all four tasks.

## 2 PRELIMINARIES

## 2.1 RECAP OF THE TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE

The transformer model (Vaswani et al., 2017) is now the workhorse architecture behind most stateof-the-art PLMs. In this section we recap the equations of this model for completeness. Transformer models are composed of _L_ stacked blocks, where each block (Figure 1) contains two types of sub-

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

layers: multi-head self-attention and a fully connected feed-forward network (FFN).[2] The conventional attention function maps queries _**Q** ∈_ R _[n][×][d][k]_ and key-value pairs _**K** ∈_ R _[m][×][d][k] ,_ _**V** ∈_ R _[m][×][d][v]_ :

where _n_ and _m_ are the number of queries and key-value pairs respectively. Multi-head attention performs the attention function in parallel over _Nh_ heads, where each head is separately parameterized by _**W** q_[(] _[i]_[)] _[,]_ _**[ W]**_[ (] _k[i]_[)] _[,]_ _**[ W]**_[ (] _v[i]_[)] _∈_ R _[d][×][d][h]_ to project inputs to queries, keys, and values. Given a sequence of _m_ vectors _**C** ∈_ R _[m][×][d]_ over which we would like to perform attention and a query vector _**x** ∈_ R _[d]_ , multi-head attention (MHA) computes the output on each head and concatenates them:[3]

where _**W** o ∈_ R _[d][×][d]_ . _d_ is the model dimension, and in MHA _dh_ is typically set to _d/Nh_ to save parameters, which indicates that each attention head is operating on a lower-dimensional space. The other important sublayer is the fully connected feed-forward network (FFN) which consists of two linear transformations with a ReLU activation function in between:

where _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][×][d][m]_ , _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[d][m][×][d]_ . Transformers typically use a large _dm_ , e.g. _dm_ = 4 _d_ . Finally, a residual connection is used followed by layer normalization (Ba et al., 2016).

## 2.2 OVERVIEW OF PREVIOUS PARAMETER-EFFICIENT TUNING METHODS

Below and in Figure 1, we introduce several state-of-the-art parameter-efficient tuning methods. Unless otherwise specified, they only tune the added parameters while the PLM’s are frozen.

**Adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019):** The adapter approach inserts small modules (adapters) between transformer layers. The adapter layer generally uses a down-projection with _**W**_ down _∈_ R _[d][×][r]_ to project the input _**h**_ to a lower-dimensional space specified by bottleneck dimension _r_ , followed by a nonlinear activation function _f_ ( _·_ ), and a up-projection with _**W**_ up _∈_ R _[r][×][d]_ . These adapters are surrounded by a residual connection, leading to a final form:

_**h** ←_ _**h**_ + _f_ ( _**hW**_ down) _**W**_ up _._ (4)

Houlsby et al. (2019) places two adapters sequentially within one layer of the transformer, one after the multi-head attention and one after the FFN sub-layer. Pfeiffer et al. (2021) have proposed a more efficient adapter variant that is inserted only after the FFN “add & layer norm” sub-layer.

**Prefix Tuning (Li & Liang, 2021):** Inspired by the success of textual prompting methods (Liu et al., 2021a), prefix tuning prepends _l_ tunable prefix vectors to the keys and values of the multihead attention at every layer. Specifically, two sets of prefix vectors _**P** k,_ _**P** v ∈_ R _[l][×][d]_ are concatenated with the original key _**K**_ and value _**V**_ . Then multi-head attention is performed on the new prefixed keys and values. The computation of head _i_ in Eq. 2 becomes:

_**P** k_ and _**P** v_ are split into _Nh_ head vectors respectively and _**P** k_[(] _[i]_[)] _[,]_ _**[ P]**_[ (] _v[i]_[)] _∈_ R _[l][×][d/N][h]_ denote the _i_ -th head vector. Prompt-tuning (Lester et al., 2021) simplifies prefix-tuning by only prepending to the input word embeddings in the first layer; similar work also includes P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021b).

**LoRA (Hu et al., 2021):** LoRA injects trainable low-rank matrices into transformer layers to approximate the weight updates. For a pre-trained weight matrix _**W** ∈_ R _[d][×][k]_ , LoRA represents its update with a low-rank decomposition _**W**_ +∆ _W_ = _**W**_ + _**W**_ down _**W**_ up, where _**W**_ down _∈_ R _[d][×][r] ,_ _**W**_ up _∈_ R _[r][×][k]_ are tunable parameters. LoRA applies this update to the query and value projection matrices ( _**W** q,_ _**W** v_ ) in the multi-head attention sub-layer, as shown in Figure 1. For a specific input _**x**_ to the linear projection in multi-head attention, LoRA modifies the projection output _**h**_ as:

computation graph of prefix tuning from this view, which allows for abstraction of prefix tuning as a plug-in module like adapters. Further, we note that _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][h][×][l]_ and _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[l][×][d][h]_ are lowrank matrices when _l_ is small, and thus they function similarly to the _**W**_ down and _**W**_ up matrices in adapters. This view also suggests that the number of prefix vectors, _l_ , plays a similar role to the bottleneck dimension _r_ in adapters: they both represent the rank limitation of computing the modification vector ∆ _**h**_ . Thus we also refer _l_ as the bottleneck dimension. Intuitively, the rank limitation implies that ∆ _**h**_ is a linear combination of _the same l_ (or _≤ l_ ) basis vectors for any _**x**_ .

**The Difference from Adapters:** In addition to the gating variable _λ_ , we emphasize three differences between prefix tuning and adapters. (1) As demonstrated in Figure 3, prefix tuning uses _**x**_ , the input of the PLM layer, to compute ∆ _**h**_ , while adapters use _**h**_ , the output of the PLM layer. Thus, prefix tuning can be thought of as a “parallel” computation to the PLM layer, whereas the typical adapter is “sequential” computation. (2) Adapters are more flexible with respect to where they are inserted than prefix tuning: adapters typically modify attention or FFN outputs, while prefix tuning only modifies the attention output of each head. Empirically, this makes a large difference as we will show in §4.4. (3) Eq. 10 applies to each attention head, while adapters are always single-headed, which makes prefix tuning more expressive: head attention is of dimension _d/Nh_ – basically we have full rank updates to each attention head if _l ≥ d/Nh_ , but we only get full-rank updates to the whole attention output with adapters if _r ≥ d_ . Notably, prefix tuning is not adding more parameters than adapters when _l_ = _r_ .[7] We empirically validate such multi-head influence in §4.4.

## 3.2 THE UNIFIED FRAMEWORK

Inspired by the connections between prefix tuning and adapters, we propose a general framework that aims to unify several state-of-the-art parameter-efficient tuning methods. Specifically, we cast them as learning a modification vector ∆ _**h**_ , which is applied to various hidden representations. Formally, we denote the hidden representation to be directly modified as _**h**_ , and the direct input to the PLM sub-module that computes _**h**_ as _**x**_ (e.g. _**h**_ and _**x**_ can be the attention output and input respectively). To characterize this modification process, we define a set of design dimensions, and different methods can be instantiated by varying values along these dimensions. We detail the design dimensions below, and illustrate how adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA fall along them in Table 1:

**Functional Form** is the specific function that computes ∆ _**h**_ . We have detailed the functional form for adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA in Eq. 4, 6, and 10 respectively. The functional forms of all these methods are similar with a proj ~~d~~ own _→_ nonlinear _→_ proj ~~u~~ p architecture, while “nonlinear” degenerates to the identity function in LoRA.

**Modified Representation** indicates which hidden representation is directly modified.[8]

**Insertion Form** is how the added module is inserted into the network. As mentioned in the previous section and shown in Figure 3, traditionally adapters are inserted at a position in a sequential manner, where both the input and output are _**h**_ . Prefix tuning and LoRA – although not originally described in this way – turn out to be equivalent to a parallel insertion where _**x**_ is the input.

> 7We will detail in §4.1 the number of parameters added of different methods.

> 8Strictly speaking, all the hidden representations would be indirectly influenced by modifying the ones before them. Here we refer to the position being _directly_ modified by the added module.

5

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**Composition Function** is how the modified vector ∆ _**h**_ is composed with the original hidden representation _**h**_ to form the new hidden representation. For example, adapters perform simple additive composition, prefix tuning uses a gated additive composition as shown in Eq. 10, and LoRA scales ∆ _**h**_ by a constant factor and adds it to the original hidden representation as in Eq. 6.

We note that many other methods not present in Table 1 fit into this framework as well. For example, prompt tuning modifies the head attention in the first layer in a way similar to prefix tuning, and various adapter variants (Pfeiffer et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2021) can be represented in a similar way as adapters. Critically, the unified framework allows us to study parameter-efficient tuning methods along these design dimensions, identify the critical design choices, and potentially transfer design elements across approaches, as in the following section.

## 3.3 TRANSFERRING DESIGN ELEMENTS

Here, and in Figure 3, we describe just a few novel methods that can be derived through our unified view above by transferring design elements across methods: (1) _Parallel Adapter_ is the variant by transferring the parallel insertion of prefix tuning into adapters. Interestingly, while we motivate the parallel adapter due to its similarity to prefix tuning, concurrent work (Zhu et al., 2021) independently proposed this variant and studied it empirically; (2) _Multi-head Parallel Adapter_ is a further step to make adapters more similar to prefix tuning: we apply parallel adapters to modify head attention outputs as prefix tuning. This way the variant improves the capacity for free by utilizing the multi-head projections as we discuss in §3.1. (3) _Scaled Parallel Adapter_ is the variant by transferring the composition and insertion form of LoRA into adapters, as shown in Figure 3e.

Our discussion and formulation so far raise a few questions: Do methods varying the design elements above exhibit distinct properties? Which design dimensions are particularly important? Do the novel methods described above yield better performance? We answer these questions next.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 GENERAL SETUP

**Datasets:** We study four downstream tasks: (1) XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) is an English summarization dataset where models predict a summary given a news article; (2) English to Romanian translation using the WMT 2016 en-ro dataset (Bojar et al., 2016); (3) MNLI (Williams et al., 2018) is an English natural language inference dataset where models predict whether one sentence entails, contradicts, or is neutral to another. (4) SST2 (Socher et al., 2013) is an English sentiment classification benchmark where models predict whether a sentence’s sentiment is positive or negative.

**Setup:** We use BARTLARGE (Lewis et al., 2020) and a multilingual version of it, mBARTLARGE (Liu et al., 2020a), as the underlying pretrained models for XSum and en-ro translation respectively, and we use RoBERTaBASE (Liu et al., 2019) for MNLI and SST2. We vary the bottleneck dimension within _{_ 1 _,_ 30 _,_ 200 _,_ 512 _,_ 1024 _}_ if needed.[9] We mainly study adapters, prefix tuning (prefix), and LoRA which greatly outperform bitfit and prompt tuning in our experiments. In the analysis sections (§4.3-4.5) we insert adapters _either_ at the attention or FFN layers for easier analysis, but include the results of inserting at both places in the final comparison (§4.6). We re-implement these methods based on their respective public code.[10] We use the huggingface transformers library (Wolf et al., 2020) for our implementation. Complete setup details can be found in Appendix A.

**Evaluation:** We report ROUGE 1/2/L scores (R-1/2/L, Lin (2004)) on the XSum test set, BLEU scores (Papineni et al., 2002) on the en-ro test set, and accuracy on the MNLI and SST2 dev set. For MNLI and SST2, we take the median of five random runs. We also report the number of tuned parameters relative to that in full fine-tuning (#params).

**Number of Tunable Parameters:** BART and mBART have an encoder-decoder structure that has three types of attention: encoder self-attention, decoder self-attention, and decoder cross-attention. RoBERTa only has encoder self-attention. For each attention sub-layer, the number of parameters

used of each method is: (1) prefix tuning prepends _l_ vectors to the keys and values and uses 2 _× l × d_ parameters; (2) adapter has _**W**_ down and _**W**_ up thus uses 2 _×r ×d_ parameters; (3) LoRA employs a pair of _**W**_ down and _**W**_ up for query and value projections, hence uses 4 _× r × d_ parameters. For the adapter modification at ffn, it uses 2 _× r × d_ parameters which is the same as adapter at attention. Therefore, for a specific value of _r_ or _l_ , prefix tuning uses the same number of parameters as adapters, while LoRA uses more parameters. More details can be found in Appendix B.

## 4.2 THE RESULTS OF EXISTING METHODS

We first overview the results of existing methods on the four tasks. As shown in Figure 4 and Table 2, while existing methods can achieve competitive performance on MNLI and SST2 by tuning fewer than 1% parameters, a large gap is still present if we add 5% parameters in XSum and en-ro. The gap remains significant even though we increase the relative parameter size to _>_ 10%. Even larger gaps have been observed in Raffel et al. (2020) on high-resource MT tasks. This shows that many methods that claimed comparable results to full fine-tuning on the GLUE benchmark with an encoder-only model (Guo et al., 2021; Ben Zaken et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2021), or on relatively simple generation benchmarks such as E2E (Novikova et al., 2017) with an encoder-decoder model (Li & Liang, 2021), may not generalize well to other standard benchmarks. The influencing factors could be complicated including the number of training samples, task complexity, or model architecture. We thus advocate for future research on this line to report results on more diverse benchmarks to exhibit a more complete picture of their performance profile. Below, our analysis will mainly focus on the XSum and en-ro datasets to better distinguish different design choices. We note that these two benchmarks are relatively high-resource performed with an encoder-decoder model (BART), while we will discuss the results on MNLI and SST2 with an encoder-only model (RoBERTa) in §4.6.

- 4.3 WHICH INSERTION FORM – SEQUENTIAL OR PARALLEL?

We first study the insertion form design dimension, comparing the proposed parallel adapter (PA) variant to the conventional sequential adapter (SA) over both the attention (att) and FFN modification. We also include prefix tuning as a reference point. As shown in Table 3, prefix tuning, which uses parallel insertion, outperforms attention sequential adapters. Further, the parallel adapter is able to beat sequential adapters in all cases,[11] with PA (ffn) outperforming SA (ffn) by 1.7 R-2 points on

## 4.4 WHICH MODIFIED REPRESENTATION – ATTENTION OR FFN?

**Setup:** We now study the effect of modifying different representations. We mainly compare attention and FFN modification. For easier analysis we categorize methods that modifies any hidden representations in the attention sub-layer (e.g. the head output, query, etc) as modifying the attention module. We compare parallel adapters at attention and FFN and prefix tuning. We also transfer the FFN modification to LoRA to have a LoRA (ffn) variant for a complete comparison. Specifically, we use LoRA to approximate the parameter updates for the FFN weights _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][×][d][m]_ and _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[d][m][×][d]_ . In this case _**W**_ up in LoRA for _**W**_ 1 (similar for _**W**_ down of _**W**_ 2) would have dimensions of _r × dm_ , where _dm_ = 4 _d_ as described in §2.1. Thus we typically use smaller _r_ for LoRA (ffn) than other methods to match their overall parameter size in later experiments.

**Results:** As shown in Figure 5, _any_ method with FFN modification outperforms _all_ the methods with attention modification in all cases (the red markers are generally above all the blue ones, the only exception is ffn-PA with 2.4% params), often with fewer parameters. Second, the same method applied at FFN always improves over its attention counterpart. For example, LoRA (ffn) improves LoRA (attn) by 1 R-2 points on XSum. We also highlight that prefix tuning does not keep improving when we further increase the capacity, which is also observed in Li & Liang (2021). These results suggest that _FFN modification can utilize the added parameters more effectively than attention, no matter what the functional form or composition function is_ . We hypothesize that this is because the FFN learns task-specific textual patterns (Geva et al., 2021), while attention learns pairwise positional interactions which do not require large capacity for adapting to new tasks.

**Is the story different when we use** 0 _._ 1% **parameters?** In §3.1 we reason that prefix tuning is more expressive than adapters (attn), which, however, is not reflected in Figure 5. We conjecture that this is because multi-head attention is only superior when the parameter budget is small. To validate this hypothesis, we compare prefix tuning to parallel adapters when they add 0 _._ 1% of the pretrained parameters. To ablate the impact of the composition function, we also report the results of removing the gating in prefix tuning as _**h**_ + ∆ _**h**_ . We include the results of the multi-head parallel adapter variant (MH PA) described in §3.3. As shown in Table 4, the multi-head methods – prefix tuning and MH PA (attn) – outperform all others by at least 1.6 BLEU points when using 0.1% of the parameters. Surprisingly, reducing _l_ from 200 to 30 only causes 0.4 BLEU loss for prefix tuning while PA (attn) loses 1.9 points. The gating composition function in prefix tuning slightly helps the results by 0.3 points. We highlight that the MH parallel adapter improves the single-headed version by 1.6 points, which again verifies the effectiveness of the multi-head formulation.

Combining the results in Figure 5 and Table 4, we conclude that _modifying head attention shows the best results when the parameter budget is very small, while the FFN can better utilize modifications at larger capacities._ This suggests that it may be effective to allocate a larger parameter budget to FFN modification instead of treating attention and FFN equally as in Houlsby et al. (2019).

## 4.5 WHICH COMPOSITION FUNCTION?

We have presented three composition functions in §3.2: simple addition (adapter), gated addition (prefix tuning) and scaled addition (LoRA). As it is unnatural to incorporate the exact gated addition into methods whose functional form does not use softmax, we examine the other two by

The reason may be the different libraries which the training code is based on – full fine-tuning is very sensitive to training hyperparameters. ## 4.6 AN EFFECTIVE INTEGRATION BY TRANSFERRING FAVORABLE DESIGN ELEMENTS

We first highlight three findings in previous sections: (1) Scaled parallel adapter is the best variant to modify FFN; (2) FFN can better utilize modification at larger capacities; and (3) modifying head attentions like prefix tuning can achieve strong performance with only 0.1% parameters. Inspired by them, we mix and match the favorable designs behind these findings: specifically, we use prefix tuning with a small bottleneck dimension ( _l_ = 30) at the attention sub-layers and allocate more parameter budgets to modify FFN representation using the scaled parallel adapter ( _r_ = 512). Since prefix tuning can be viewed as a form of adapter in our unified framework, we name this variant as _Mix-And-Match adapter (MAM Adapter)_ . In Table 6, we compare MAM adapter with various parameter-efficient tuning methods. For completeness, we also present results of other combination versions in Table 6: using parallel adapters at both attention and FFN layers and combining prefix tuning (attn) with LoRA (ffn) – both of these combined versions can improve over their respective prototypes. However, MAM Adapter achieves the best performance on both tasks and is able to match the results of our full fine-tuning by only updating 6 _._ 7% of the pre-trained parameters. In Table 2, we present the results of MAM Adapter on MNLI and SST2 as well, where MAM Adapter achieves comparable results to full fine-tuning by adding only 0.5% of pretrained parameters.

## 5 DISCUSSION

We provide a unified framework for several performant parameter-tuning methods, which enables us to instantiate a more effective model that matches the performance of full fine-tuning method through transferring techniques across approaches. We hope our work can provide insights and guidance for future research on parameter-efficient tuning.

9

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## ETHICS STATEMENT

Our work proposes a method for efficient fine-tuning of pre-trained models, in particular language models. Pre-trained language models have a wide variety of positive applications, such as the applications to summarization, translation, or language understanding described in our paper. At the same time, there are a number of ethical concerns with language models in general, including concerns regarding the generation of biased or discriminative text (Bordia & Bowman, 2019), the leakage of private information from training data (Carlini et al., 2020), and environmental impact of training or tuning them (Strubell et al., 2019).

Our method attempts to train language models making minimal changes to their pre-existing parameters. While it is an interesting research question whether parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods exacerbate, mitigate, or make little change to issues such as bias or information leakage, to our knowledge no previous work has examined this topic. It is an interesting avenue for future work.

With respect to environmental impact, the methods proposed in this paper add a small number of extra parameters and components to existing models, and thus they have a nominal negative impact on training and inference time – for example, the final MAM Adapter needs 100% - 150% training time of full fine-tuning in our four benchmarks since parameter-efficient tuning typically needs more epochs to converge; the inference time is roughly the same as the model obtained by full fine-tuning. On the other hand, as the methods proposed in this paper may obviate the need for full fine-tuning, this may also significantly reduce the cost (in terms of memory/deployed servers) of serving models. Notably, the great majority of the experimentation done for this paper was performed on a data center powered entirely by renewable energy.

## REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

In addition to the setup description in §4.1, we have detailed the complete experiments setup such as batch size, optimizer, learning rates in Appendix A. Besides, we have publicized our source code. These resources should be sufficient to reproduce results of the paper.

## ACKNOWLEDGEMENT

We thank the anonymous reviewers for their comments. This work was supported in part by the CMU-Portugal MAIA Project, a Baidu PhD Fellowship for Junxian He, and a CMU Presidential Fellowship for Chunting Zhou.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper provides a valuable and influential unified framework for parameter-efficient transfer learning (PETL). It successfully decomposes several disparate methods (Adapters, Prefix Tuning, LoRA) into a shared design space of functional forms, insertion forms, and composition functions. The empirical analysis is robust, covering diverse tasks (summarization, translation, classification) and correctly identifying that FFN modification scales better while attention modification is efficient at very low parameter budgets. However, while the high-level logic is sound and the resulting 'MAM Adapter' is effective, there are minor technical ambiguities regarding the implementation of LoRA-style modifications to the FFN and specific notation used in the derivations.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling unified framework for parameter-efficient transfer learning methods, effectively reinterpreting adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA through a shared lens of hidden state modifications. The experimental design is thorough, with careful ablation studies across multiple NLP tasks that convincingly support the main claims. The proposed MAM Adapter variant demonstrates strong performance with minimal parameters, aligning with the paper's narrative. However, several technical inconsistencies in equations and parameter counting—particularly around LoRA and composition functions—raise concerns about reproducibility and precision. While most issues are localized and fixable, one major discrepancy in the number of trainable parameters for LoRA could affect cross-method comparisons and weaken the paper's empirical conclusions if uncorrected.

comments.title: Incorrect LoRA parameter count in text versus standard practice
comments.quote: LoRA employs a pair of _**W**_ down and _**W**_ up for query and value projections, hence uses 4 _× r × d_ parameters.
comments.explanation: The text claims LoRA uses 4 × r × d parameters because it applies low-rank updates to both query and value projections, each requiring _**W**_ down ∈ ℝ^(d×r) and _**W**_ up ∈ ℝ^(r×d). However, this is incorrect: when applied to both query and value matrices, LoRA introduces two separate low-rank pairs—one for _**W**_ q and one for _**W**_ v—each contributing r×d + d×r ≈ 2rd parameters, totaling approximately 4rd. But in standard LoRA (and as confirmed in Hu et al., 2021), _r_ is much smaller than _d_, so this is accurate only if both matrices are updated. The issue is that the paper later compares methods using the same _r_, implying fair parameter budgets, but fails to clarify that LoRA’s actual parameter count is about twice that of an adapter (2rd) for the same _r_. This affects comparisons in Figure 5 and Table 6 where 'LoRA (attn)' is compared to 'PA (attn)' at similar _r_ values without adjusting for double the parameters. While Table 4 does attempt to match parameter sizes, the main text presents this claim without qualification, potentially misleading readers into thinking LoRA is more parameter-efficient than it is. This undermines fair benchmarking and is a real issue even if due to imprecise explanation rather than calculation error.

comments.title: Missing scaling factor in prefix tuning composition function
comments.explanation: In Table 1 and Section 3.2, the composition function for prefix tuning is given as a gated addition with λ. However, in Equation (10), λ is defined as softmax(_**xW**_1)_**W**_2 and then used directly in the composition. But no mechanism is specified for constraining λ to [0,1], which is essential for a valid interpolation. In fact, in the original prefix tuning (Li & Liang, 2021), the gating is implemented via a sigmoid, not softmax followed by a linear transform, to ensure numerical stability and proper gating behavior. Here, _**W**_2 is a learned matrix, so λ could take arbitrary real values, making (1−λ) potentially negative or greater than 1. This would break the gating interpretation and could lead to unstable training. The authors may intend λ to be element-wise clamped or passed through a sigmoid, but this is not stated. While this might be resolved in code, the formal specification is underspecified and ambiguous enough to prevent correct reproduction.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for bottleneck dimension in prefix tuning and adapters
comments.quote: we also refer _l_ as the bottleneck dimension.
comments.explanation: The paper equates _l_, the number of prefix vectors in prefix tuning, with the bottleneck dimension _r_ in adapters, arguing both control the rank of ∆_**h**_. While this is conceptually useful, it is mathematically imprecise: in prefix tuning, the effective rank of the modification is bounded by _l_, but in adapters it is bounded by _r_. However, the number of parameters differs—prefix tuning uses 2ld, adapters use 2rd—so setting _l_ = _r_ yields equal parameters, which the authors leverage. This is correct and consistent in parameter counting (e.g., Section 4.1), so the notation is used purposefully to enable comparison. The potential confusion is resolved by the context, particularly in Table 3 where _l_=200 and _r_=200 are compared directly with matching parameter percentages. Thus, while initially concerning, the conflation is justified by design and not an error.

comments.title: Ambiguity in LoRA's composition function scaling factor _s_
comments.quote: LoRA scales ∆_**h**_ by a constant factor and adds it to the original hidden representation as in Eq. 6.
comments.explanation: Equation (6) shows _**h** ← **h** + s ⋅ (**xW**_down_**W**_up_), but the origin of _s_ is not defined. The footnote mentions that the public LoRA code uses different _s_ per dataset and that it affects results significantly, yet the main text does not specify whether _s_ is fixed, learned, or how it's chosen. In Table 5, results are reported for _s_=4 and _s_=1, suggesting it's a hyperparameter, but no justification is given for why _s_=4 is preferred. This is problematic because scaling can functionally mimic learning rate or initialization effects. Hu et al. (2021) originally proposed _s_ = α/_r_ to stabilize training, but this paper never defines this normalization. Without specifying _s_, the method is underspecified, making reproduction difficult. The authors do test trainable _s_ and find it worse, but still do not justify their default value. This is a real gap in method description.

comments.title: Mismatch between figure and text in XSum full fine-tuning performance
comments.explanation: In Figure 2, the full fine-tuning baseline is plotted at approximately 21.94 ROUGE-2, which matches the text value. However, in Figure 4 (left), the full fine-tuning marker is at or below 20.98, which contradicts both. More precisely, the y-axis in Figure 4 goes up to ~22, but the full fine-tuning marker appears around 20.98—the same value as the adapter result in the legend. This creates confusion: is full fine-tuning really only 20.98? Cross-referencing with Table 6, which reports 21.94, confirms the figure is mislabeled. The marker labeled 'Full Fine-tuning' in Figure 4 likely corresponds to 'Adapter' from Figure 1, suggesting a label mix-up. This could mislead readers about the performance gap. Given that Figure 4 is central to the paper’s empirical claims, this is a serious issue. However, since the correct number appears in the text and Table 6, and the relative trends hold, the overall conclusion is not undermined.

comments.title: Underspecified multi-head parallel adapter implementation
comments.quote: Multi-head Parallel Adapter ... apply parallel adapters to modify head attention outputs
comments.explanation: The method is described conceptually but lacks formal definition. Do parallel adapters operate on each head's output before concatenation? Are _**W**_ down and _**W**_ up shared across heads or head-specific? If applied per head, the input dimension would be _d/Nh_, so _**W**_ down ∈ ℝ^(d/Nh × r), changing parameter count entirely. The paper does not specify. In Table 1, it uses the same functional form as adapter with ReLU(_**hW**_down)_**W**_up, implying _h_ is the full hidden state, not per-head. But this contradicts 'modified representation: head attn'. This ambiguity prevents reproduction. The authors validate it empirically in Table 4, showing gains at 0.1%, but without implementation details, it's unclear how the modification interacts with multi-head structure. This is especially important given their claim about multi-head expressiveness in Section 3.1.

comments.title: Overstatement of performance matching full fine-tuning across all tasks
comments.quote: achieving comparable results to fine-tuning all parameters on all four tasks
comments.explanation: The abstract claims MAM Adapter matches full fine-tuning on all four tasks. However, in Table 2, MAM Adapter achieves 87.4 on MNLI vs 87.6 for full fine-tuning—a small gap, but still below. On SST2, it matches 94.2, but full FT is 94.6. This is a 0.4-point gap, which is not trivial given the standard deviations (~±0.3). While the paper says 'comparable', the abstract implies equivalence. In MT and XSum, MAM Adapter reaches or exceeds their full FT numbers, but those are 'our run' and likely not fully optimized. The full FT_†_ from prior work is higher (37.7 BLEU vs 37.5). So the method does not quite match full fine-tuning on all tasks. This overstates the empirical achievement, though the results are still impressive. Given that the core contribution is the framework and MAM is a derivation, the claim is mildly overstated but not misleading in context.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a valuable unified framework for parameter-efficient transfer learning methods, offering insights into the connections between adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA. The work is well-structured with comprehensive experiments across multiple tasks. However, there are some technical issues that need attention, particularly regarding the derivation of prefix tuning's alternative form and unsubstantiated claims about training time efficiency.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for prefix tuning derivation
comments.quote: which reaches a very similar form to the adapter function in Eq. 4, except that prefix tuning is performing weighted addition while the adapter one is unweighted... h ← (1−λ)h + λ∆h where ∆h = softmax(xW_q P_k^⊤) P_v
comments.explanation: The paper claims to derive an alternative form of prefix tuning that reveals connections with adapters, but the derivation from Eq. 5 to Eq. 10 is non-trivial and not shown. Starting from the standard prefix tuning formulation that prepends vectors to keys and values, reaching the gated additive form requires mathematical steps that are not explained. This makes it difficult to verify the claimed equivalence and understand the exact relationship between prefix tuning and adapters under this view.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated training time claim
comments.quote: the final MAM Adapter needs 100% - 150% training time of full fine-tuning in our four benchmarks since parameter-efficient tuning typically needs more epochs to converge
comments.explanation: The paper makes a specific quantitative claim about training time efficiency without providing supporting evidence. No experiments, timing comparisons, or ablation studies are presented to validate the 100%-150% range. This claim appears in the Ethics Statement where it has implications for environmental impact assessment, making its verification particularly important. Without empirical backing, this statement remains an unsubstantiated assertion.

comments.title: Inconsistent scaling factor notation
comments.quote: LoRA (s = 4) performs better than parallel adapter. However, the advantage disappears if we remove the scaling by setting s = 1
comments.explanation: The scaling factor s is introduced without formal definition in the main text. While it's mentioned in Table 5 caption that s is selected based on dev set performance, the exact role of s in the LoRA formulation (Eq. 6) is unclear. In Eq. 6, s appears as a multiplicative factor, but its relationship to hyperparameter selection and optimization needs clearer specification to ensure reproducibility.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper provides a thoughtful and cohesive framework for understanding parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, successfully bridging the conceptual gap between disparate approaches like adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA. The methodology for deconstructing these methods into design dimensions (functional form, insertion, representation, composition) is well-justified. The empirical section is thorough, and the synthesis of findings results in a genuinely improved 'MAM adapter' method. While there are some minor notational ambiguities and a slightly confusing passage regarding the 'multi-head influence' of prefix tuning, the technical contributions are clear and the experimental validation is robust.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'multi-head influence' argument
comments.quote: Notably, prefix tuning is not adding more parameters than adapters when $l = r$.[7] ... Eq. 10 applies to each attention head, while adapters are always single-headed, which makes prefix tuning more expressive: head attention is of dimension $d/Nh$ – basically we have full rank updates to each attention head if $l \ge d/Nh$, but we only get full-rank updates to the whole attention output with adapters if $r \ge d$.
comments.explanation: The authors argue that prefix tuning is more 'expressive' because it applies updates per-head (in the projection space of $d/Nh$) rather than to the whole concatenated output (in the $d$ space). While it is true that prefix tuning updates keys and values per head, the claim that it achieves 'full-rank updates to each attention head' if $l \ge d/Nh$ is slightly misleading because the total parameter count per layer in prefix tuning is $2 \cdot l \cdot d$ (shared across heads), whereas if a hypothetical adapter were applied per head with bottleneck $r_{head}$, it would have $Nh \cdot 2 \cdot r_{head} \cdot (d/Nh) = 2 \cdot r_{head} \cdot d$. Mathematically, these are the same parameter budget if $l=r_{head}$. The prefix tuning approach inherently shares parameters across heads ($P_k, P_v$), whereas standard adapters treat the hidden state as a single vector. I followed the math and checked the parameter counts, and while the result holds, the phrasing risks confusing 'functional expressivity' with 'rank constraint per head,' which is a function of the concatenation design rather than just the math.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in Equations
comments.explanation: In Section 2.1, the authors define $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times dm}$ and $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{dm \times d}$. Later, in Section 4.4, they refer to $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times dm}$ and $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{dm \times d}$ again when describing LoRA for FFN, but usually, FFN layers have an intermediate dimension denoted as $d_{ff}$ or similar. Given that $dm$ is defined as $4d$ in the text, the notation is self-consistent within the paper, but using $dm$ as an index/dimension variable alongside $d$ (model dimension) is slightly unconventional and could be jarring to a reader accustomed to $d_{ff}$ notation. Context makes it clear, so it does not impede understanding.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'relative' parameter count
comments.quote: The number of fine-tuned parameters is relative to the tuned parameters in full fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The figure caption (Fig 2) and text define parameter counting as 'relative to full fine-tuning.' This is usually clear, but there is ambiguity in how 'full fine-tuning' parameter counts are handled for Encoder-Decoder models (like BART) vs Decoder-only. The authors clarify in Appendix B (referenced in the text) but the main text doesn't explicitly state whether this includes embedding layers or just transformer blocks. A careful reader would need to cross-check Appendix B to confirm if the percentages are strictly comparable across different model architectures (RoBERTa vs BART). This is a minor framing concern as the relative efficiency trends remain valid regardless of absolute inclusion.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper offers a conceptually appealing unified framework for parameter-efficient transfer learning, and the ablation studies provide useful insights into design dimensions. However, the derivation of prefix tuning as a convex combination of two attention outputs contains a critical mathematical error: the softmax normalization is incorrectly factored, making the claimed equivalence invalid. This undermines the theoretical foundation of the unified view. Otherwise, the experimental design and comparisons are generally sound.

comments.title: Mathematical error in deriving prefix tuning as a convex combination of attentions
comments.quote:
First, we note that the attention with prefix in Eq. 5 can be written as:
head_i = softmax( xW_q^{(i)} [C W_k^{(i)}; P_k^{(i)}]^⊤ ) [C W_v^{(i)}; P_v^{(i)}]
where the two parts are separated based on the partition:
head_i = softmax( xW_q^{(i)} (C W_k^{(i)})^⊤ ; xW_q^{(i)} P_k^{(i)⊤}) [C W_v^{(i)}; P_v^{(i)}]
Denoting ... it becomes: ...
comments.explanation: The derivation assumes that the softmax applied to the concatenated key matrix can be separated into two independent softmax operations applied to the prefix and original parts individually. However, softmax normalizes over the entire concatenated dimension, so the output is not a simple convex combination of the two separate attention outputs. The scalar λ derived from the partition is input-dependent and does not factor as shown. This mistake invalidates the claimed equivalence between prefix tuning and a gated adapter, which is a central insight of the paper's unified framework.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement about FFN vs attention comparison
comments.quote: any method with FFN modification outperforms all the methods with attention modification in all cases (the red markers are generally above all the blue ones, the only exception is ffn-PA with 2.4% params)
comments.explanation: The phrase "the only exception is ffn-PA with 2.4% params" contradicts the preceding claim that FFN methods outperform all attention methods in all cases. The intended meaning is likely that the attention-based method (not FFN) is the exception, or the wording is a typo. This could confuse a careful reader about which specific result the exception refers to.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a coherent unified framework for parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, effectively decomposing adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA into functional, positional, and compositional dimensions. The empirical analysis is thorough, and the proposed MAM Adapter variant demonstrates strong results, matching full fine-tuning on multiple benchmarks with significantly fewer parameters. However, there are a few localized inconsistencies: a numerical discrepancy in the reported improvement magnitude, a contradictory statement regarding FFN vs. attention performance at low parameter budgets, and a misattribution of the 'weighted addition' property in the composition function discussion. These issues are primarily presentational or refer to external appendix data, but they require clarification to ensure precise communication of the framework's insights and experimental findings.

comments.title: Numerical discrepancy in reported improvement magnitude
comments.quote: PA (ffn) outperforming SA (ffn) by 1.7 R-2 points on XSum
comments.explanation: In Section 4.3, the authors state that parallel adapters (PA) outperform sequential adapters (SA) by 1.7 R-2 points. However, Table 3 reports SA(ffn) at 19.98 R-2 and PA(ffn) at 20.66 R-2, which is a difference of 0.68 points. The authors note in a footnote that 'more results with different r can be found in Appendix C,' suggesting the 1.7 value may come from a different bottleneck dimension or setting. As written, the text contradicts the provided table, which could mislead readers about the magnitude of the gain.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about FFN vs. Attention performance
comments.quote: any method with FFN modification outperforms all the methods with attention modification in all cases (the red markers are generally above all the blue ones, the only exception is ffn-PA with 2.4% params)
comments.explanation: The text claims that FFN modification always outperforms attention modification, then lists 'ffn-PA with 2.4% params' as an exception. This is confusing because Table 3 shows PA(ffn) at 2.4% parameters achieves an R-2 of 20.66, which is strictly higher than PA(attn) at 3.6% parameters (20.31) and SA(attn) at 3.6% (20.31). The data does not support FFN-PA being an exception where attention performs better. The intended message likely relates to the saturation or capacity thresholds discussed in the conclusion, but the specific phrasing contradicts the reported numbers.

comments.title: Misattribution of composition function property
comments.quote: which reaches a very similar form to the adapter function in Eq. 4, except that prefix tuning is performing weighted addition while the adapter one is unweighted.[6]
comments.explanation: This sentence appears in Section 3.1 immediately after deriving the functional form of LoRA (`h <- h + W_down W_up x`). It seems intended to contrast LoRA's scaled addition (via factor s) with the adapter's unweighted additive composition. However, it mistakenly attributes the 'weighted addition' to prefix tuning instead of LoRA. Given that Table 1 and Section 3.2 clearly assign gating/scaling to prefix tuning and LoRA separately, this is likely a typo or labeling error that temporarily obscures the authors' comparison of composition functions.

comments.title: Terminological imprecision in Table 1 modified representation column
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the 'modified representation' for LoRA is listed as 'attn key/val'. Technically, LoRA (Hu et al., 2021) applies low-rank updates to the query and value *projection weight matrices* ($W_q, W_v$), not directly to the key/value tensors themselves. While the computational graph is parallel and the effect is additive to the output, describing it as modifying the key/value representation conflates LoRA with prefix tuning (which prepends vectors to actual key/value sequences). This is a minor terminological inaccuracy that does not undermine the unified framework but reduces technical precision.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a valuable theoretical framework for parameter-efficient transfer learning by unifying methods like Prefix Tuning, Adapters, and LoRA. The discussion on insertion forms and composition functions provides insightful analysis. However, the paper contains significant numerical inconsistencies regarding the parameter counts of the fine-tuned modules relative to the full model size (BART-LARGE). Reported values in the tables (e.g., 3.6% for Prefix Tuning with l=200) are mathematically implausible when calculated against the stated model dimensions ($d=1024$), and the justification for matching parameter sizes between methods in the ablation studies is flawed. These errors undermine the empirical rigor of the presented comparisons.
